title: UX Widget States and API Functions
breadcrumbs: UX Widget States and API Functions
text: UX Widget States and API Functions The following sections explain various UX widget states and it's API functions. UX widget states Introduction Widgets provide the glue between Uniface data objects (such as fields, occurrences, and DSP instances) and the browser's Document Object Model (DOM). During the life cycle of a Dynamic Server Page (DSP) instance, the data within occurrences and fields must be displayed, updated, interacted with, removed, and replaced as needed: When data needs to be shown, widgets are created to handle this. When a field value needs to be updated after a server roundtrip, widgets need to respond to this and refresh their data. When the user has made a change to a value, widgets need to fire events allowing Uniface to update the data objects on the server. When the user pushes a button, widgets need to fire events allowing Uniface to fire a trigger. When the server returns a new data set, widgets need to show this new data set and no longer show the previous data set. The average layout of a modern User Experience (UX) is rich in functionality and requires a large DOM. Creating and deleting DOM nodes is an intensive process which can have a negative impact on performance and responsiveness of the page. To improve the user experience, the widgets in DSP's reuse as many DOM nodes as possible. This adds to the complexity of UX widgets and their state. This page explains the UX widget states and API functions, needed when you create a UX widget that is performant and works properly within the DSP. Synopsis A UX widget can be in four main states: Definition only Created widget Connected widget Data-ready widget Definition Only Before any UX widget objects are created, Uniface calls the static processLayout() API function of the UX widget class. This function returns the layout of the widget and is fired only once per UX widget definition. This allows Uniface to clone the layout instead of recreating it with multiple calls to processLayout() , because cloning an object is much faster. Created Widget Uniface requires a UX widget object to show a data object. Uniface checks for and will reuse any unused UX widget. Otherwise, a new widget object is created by calling its constructor() API function. The created object is not able to serve a data object yet and is not yet connected to a DOM node. Connected Widget Before a UX widget is bound to a data object, the onConnect() API function is called. A connected widget is the object connected to a DOM node and ready to be bound. Triggers and other event listeners can now be added to the DOM with onConnect() and mapTrigger() . Data-ready Widget When Uniface binds a connected UX widget to a data object (field, occurrence, UX instance), the widget becomes a Data-ready Widget . In this state the widget is ready for updates , user interaction, and able to fire triggers. Examples This section describes, for a few examples use cases, how the various API functions are used. To avoid confusion about the term "occurrence", we will use two new terms: A "uniface-occurrence" is an occurrence of some entity, something that is related to a row of a table in the database. A "dsp-occurrence" is a part of a DSP page, the part that shows the content of a "uniface-occurrence". To illustrate the difference, consider the following, where a DSP shows information about persons. At some points in the DSP's lifetime, a dsp-occurrence might show the content of one uniface-occurrence, and later on it might show the content of a different uniface-occurrence. For example, in a DSP that has a single dsp-occurrence of a Person entity, at one point the dsp-occurrence shows fields of the uniface-occurrence for person "Jack". Later, for example, after the user clicked a "Next" button, that same dsp-occurrence shows fields of a different uniface-occurrence, for person "John". Use case: DSP page load When the browser initially receives the DSP's HTML, the static processLayout() is called for all fields that have a UX widget. The DSP's HTML is updated with what those function calls return. Then Uniface starts the process of getting UX widgets in the Data-ready state. For each field, Uniface creates the UX widget, calling the constructor of the UX widget class. Then it calls onConnect() to connect the UX widget to the HTML element that was returned by processLayout() earlier. It also calls mapTrigger() , for each trigger that the widget may need to map an event to. The UX widgets are now connected to fields in a dsp-occurrence. However, they are not yet data-ready. Uniface then calls dataInit() to set the UX widget in a state where it can receive field data from Uniface and where the end user can interact with it. Then Uniface supplies the field data (including its properties) to the widget by calling the dataUpdate() API function. Use case: binding to different field data object When a different uniface-occurrence is loaded into a given dsp-occurrence, the UX widgets in that dsp-occurrence need to bind to different field data objects. As a first step in this process, Uniface calls dataCleanup() . Now the UX widget is no longer bound to the field data object that it was representing. Then Uniface makes the UX widget data-ready for the new field data object by calling dataInit() and after that dataUpdate() , to supply the field data (including its properties) to the UX widget. Use case: trigger execution When some event leads to the execution of some trigger, Uniface first calls the blockUI() API function, to tell the widget that it should prevent the end user from interacting with it – as this could cause race conditions. For field widgets, it then calls getValue() and validate() to validate the widget's value. For entity and component widgets, validation is not performed. After the request has resulted in a response, Uniface calls dataUpdate() to supply the new field data to the UX widget. This only happens if there were changes in the field data. Depending on whether there was an error, Uniface calls showError() or hideError() . Then unblockUI() is called to let the widget allow user interaction again. Widget states and API functions in detail Static layout preparation of the DSP When Uniface prepares the static layout of the DSP, the actual UX widgets don't exist yet; they are in the Definition Only state, so to speak. In this state the framework calls the static processLayout() of the UX widget classes that will be used in the DSP. The browser receives the DSP page When a browser requests a DSP, the Uniface server returns an HTML page that is a copy of the DSP layout. Before returning that page to the browser, the Uniface server references the widgets required JavaScript modules, JavaScript sources and CSS files in the <head> of the page. To add these references in an HTML <head> , the server: Scans through all fields that are in the DSP's component structure, to get a complete list of the names of the logical DSP widgets of those fields. Determines a list of names of physical DSP widgets, using the list of logical widget names and the relevant definitions from the [webwidgets] section in the usys.in. Lists all JavaScript and CSS files, using the of physical widget names and the relevant definitions from the web.ini file. Based on these lists the server adds references to the HTML <head> section, such as: <script type="module" src= "{javascript-module-name} "></script> for the JavaScript modules that are specified for the logical widgets. <script type="text/javascript" src= "{javascript-source-name}" charset="UTF-8"></script> for the JavaScript sources that are specified for the logical widgets. <link type="text/css" rel="stylesheet" href=" {css-file-name} " /> for the CSS files that are specified for the logical widgets. With this, the static HTML of the DSP page is ready, with the <head> section holding all necessary references. The JavaScript framework processes the DSP page layout The Uniface DSP framework processes the static HTML, when the browser has loaded the DSP page and processes HTML elements bound to entities, occurrences and fields. For each HTML element bound to a Uniface field with a UX widget, the framework passes this HTML element to the static processLayout() of the UX widget class. The HTML element is replaced with the element returned by processLayout() . The UX widget class determines the initial HTML layout, and this is determined only once during the DSP's lifetime. The HTML body of the DSP page now contains a skeleton layout, with the layout fields determined by UX widget classes. Note: At this point there are still no UX widget objects yet. Creation of the UX widget When Uniface renders a dsp-occurrence for the first time, it iterates its fields. For each field that is defined to have a UX widget as its DSP widget, it creates a UX widget object, calling the UX widget's constructor. The UX widgets cease to exist when the DSP page is exited. Connecting to HTML elements When Uniface renders a dsp-occurrence, it iterates its field and connects the UX widget objects to HTML elements. For each field, it connects the UX widget to the HTML element returned by processLayout() by calling onConnect() . When Uniface creates additional dsp-occurrences, it clones the occurrence as it exists after the Static Layout Preparation phase. Uniface passes a copy of the skeleton HTML element as argument for the onConnect() calls for UX widgets of fields of such additional dsp-occurrences. When Uniface has called onConnect() , the widget is considered to be a Connected Widget. Immediately after the UX widget has been connected to an HTML element, Uniface calls mapTrigger() , once for each public web trigger that is defined for the field. Binding to and unbinding from a field data object Binding When Uniface fills a dsp-occurrence with the data from a uniface-occurrence, it calls dataInit() , to indicate that the UX widget is about to be bound to new field data. After that, the widget has become data-ready. Then Uniface calls dataUpdate() to pass the field's data to those UX widgets. In this state, the widget is ready for user interaction and for data updates by means of requests to execute Uniface triggers. Unbinding When the framework is about to reuse a dsp-occurrence for the data of a different uniface-occurrence, it calls dataCleanup() , to indicate that the widget is being unbound from the field data. Now the widget is no longer data-ready; it is only a connected widget. Updates and changes While a dsp-occurrence is in use for a specific uniface-occurrence – that is, its UX widgets are in the Data-ready state – the end user might trigger some request, which will either be executed on the server (for a trigger that is defined as "public web") or on the browser (for a webtrigger). Before the framework executes the request, Uniface calls the following API functions of the UX widgets in the dsp-occurrence: blockUI() : To tell the widget that it should block the user from interacting with the widget while the request is being processed. getValue() : To get the widget's data and send it to the server as part of the request. If the trigger specifies that it does not have input scope, then this API function is not called. validate() : To give the widget opportunity to do its own validation. Note: This validation takes place before Uniface itself performs validation. If the widget's validate() API function returns an error, then the request is aborted. If all validations succeed, Uniface executes the request. When the framework receives the response to the request, it calls the following API functions of the UX widgets in the dsp-occurrence: dataUpdate() : To let the widget know about changes to the field's data object. The framework does not call this API function if there are no changes to the field's data object. showError() or hideError() : Depending on whether there is an error in the field's data. getValue() To get the field's value. The framework this in order to be able to detect whether the field's value has changed because of user interaction. unblockUI() : To tell the widget to re-enable user interaction, as the request has been processed Widget States Quick Reference Widget State Description API Functions Called Widget Capabilities Next State Definition Only Static layout phase, no widget objects exist processLayout() (static) Layout generation only Created Widget Created Widget Widget object created but not connected to DOM constructor() Object initialization Connected Widget Connected Widget Connected to DOM, ready for data binding onConnect() , mapTrigger() Event mapping, DOM setup Data-ready Widget Data-ready Widget Bound to data object, fully functional dataInit() , dataUpdate() User interaction, triggers Connected Widget 1 Additional API Functions in Data-ready State Operation Field Widgets Entity/Component Widgets Purpose User interation All APIs available blockUI() , unblockUI() , dataUpdate() Handle user actions Server operations blockUI() , getValue() , validate() , unblockUI() blockUI() , unblockUI() Process server triggers Error handling showError() , hideError() Not applicable Display validation errors Data changes dataUpdate() dataUpdate() Update widget with new data 1 Data-ready widgets can transition back to Connected Widget state during data rebinding via dataCleanup() . Related Links APIs: DSP UX Widget Class UX Widget Class APIs allow users to build a consistent UX/UI. It opens up parts of Uniface needed to efficiently interact with the core of Uniface, while providing proper isolation of concerns keeping the development of custom constructs easy and maintainable.
